title: Sluice Gates
desc: The reservoir feeds half the hive by channels, which means the gate line needs attention before the day's draw begins.

steps.text:
The reservoir does not simply sit beneath Mount Pillar. It moves through it. Narrow stone channels carry water outward to the fungal gardens, while separate warm lines bleed carefully measured heat toward the nursery chambers. All of that begins at the sluice wall, where iron-toothed gates divide still water into useful flow. If the wall falls out of tune, the hive notices by afternoon.

The gate tender hands you a scraper and a gauge rod. "Garden line three is dragging from mineral scale," she says. "Nursery line two is taking more warmth than the chart allows. Scrape the gate teeth if you want dirty work, or rebalance the split between the warm channels if you like numbers more than sludge."

options.label: Scrape the mineral scale from the gate teeth
Rebalance the warm-water split to the nursery lines

steps.text:
Scale builds where clean engineering meets mineral-rich water and loses the argument. The teeth of garden line three are crusted pale and stiff enough that the gate no longer seats smoothly, which makes every adjustment a grind instead of a slide. You scrape it clean section by section, knocking the deposit into a catch pan while the tender works the wheel just enough to expose the next jammed edge.

Once the teeth are clear, the gate settles with a satisfying iron click and the channel current straightens immediately. No more sideways chatter against the stone lip, no more uneven pull at the mouth. The tender watches the flow rod steady out and says, "Good. Water likes habits. We prefer its habits to be ours."

options.label: Dump the scale pan and leave the garden line running true

steps.text:
The nursery lines draw from the warmer side of the basin, but even there the split must be exact. Too little heat and the brood chambers chill. Too much and the vents carry damp where it does not belong. You read both gauges, compare them to the day's chart, and ease the hand wheels until line two gives up a fraction of warmth back to line one, just enough to return the balance to where the hatch keepers expect it.

Nothing visible happens at first, which is how you know the work is serious. Then the gauge rods settle into their proper colored bands and the tender lets out the breath she has been holding. "There," she says. "A small correction before breakfast and the whole hive sleeps better tonight." In a Danian system, tiny adjustments are often the difference between routine and crisis.

options.label: Mark the new settings and leave the nursery flow balanced
